query: parts of the body that are still developing at birth

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Introduction -- Factors Affecting Growth and Development
retrieved_docs.content: After birth, the environmental factors may exert either a beneficial or detrimental effect on growth. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Introduction -- Factors Affecting Growth and Development
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal health has a highly influential role in achieving growth and development. Any stimulus or insult during fetal development causes developmental adaptations that permanently change the latter part of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is a compromise in the supply of nutrients reaching the fetus in FGR SGA. To maximize the chance of survival, the fetus responds to the reduction in nutrient supply by reducing its overall size while preserving certain functions like brain growth and lung maturation and increasing red blood cell production. [16] The fetus prioritizes blood supply to more vital organs such as the brain, heart, adrenals, and placenta. There is a decrease in the total body fat, lean mass, and mineral contents in infants with severe FGR SGA, giving the neonate a wasted appearance. [17] FGR SGA is divided into symmetric and asymmetric growth restriction. Infants with symmetric FGR SGA have a proportionate reduction in all organ systems, and the growth restriction typically begins early in the gestation. The incidence of symmetric FGR SGA ranges from 38% to 45%, and factors such as first-trimester congenital infection and chromosomal abnormalities have been implicated. In asymmetric FGR SGA, which constitutes about 55% to 61% of FGR SGA cases, the growth in head circumference is preserved while the length and weight are affected. [18] [19] [20] The growth restriction in asymmetric FGR SGA begins in the late 2nd or 3rd trimesters and usually results from reduced fetal nutrients due to placental or maternal factors. Continued brain growth is prioritized. Ponderal index (PI), which is a ratio of body weight to length (PI=[weight (g) x 100]/[length (cm)]), is an important tool to detect FGR SGA, especially in infants with asymmetric FGR SGA. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Introduction -- Stages in Human Growth and Development
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal stage: Fetal health issues can have detrimental effects on postnatal growth. One-third of neonates with intrauterine growth retardation might have curtailed postnatal growth. [1] Good perinatal care is essential in promoting fetal health and, indirectly, postnatal growth. Postnatal stage: The postnatal growth and development process happens together but at different rates. The growth occurs by discontinuous saltatory spurts with a stagnant background. [2] There are 5 significant phases in human growth and development, Infancy (neonate and up to 1 year age) Toddler (1 to 5 years of age) Childhood (3 to 11 years old) - early childhood is from 3 to 8 years old, and middle childhood is from 9 to 11 years old. Adolescence or teenage (from 12 to 18 years old) Adulthood

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Introduction -- Factors Affecting Growth and Development
retrieved_docs.content: The growth and development are positively influenced by factors like parental health and genetic composition, even before conception. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The female body goes through immense changes during a pregnancy that involve all organ systems in the body. These changes result in physiology that differs from that of a non-pregnant female. Additionally, abnormalities in the development of pregnancy can lead to further complications for both mother and fetus. With the maternal mortality rate in the United States reaching almost 18 deaths per 100,000 live births in 2009, a drastic increase from the 7.2 deaths per 100,000 in 1987, it has become more critical for all healthcare providers to understand the typical changes that accompany pregnancy, as well as recognize changes that go beyond typical pregnancy symptoms. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy is a state of having implanted products of conception located either in the uterus or elsewhere in the body. It ends through either spontaneous or elective abortion or delivery. During this time, the mother’s body goes through immense changes involving all organ systems to sustain the growing fetus. All medical providers must be aware of these alterations present in pregnancy to be able to provide the best possible care for both mother and fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Umbilical Cord -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: By week seven, the intestines begin to herniate out of the embryo through the umbilical ring and into the umbilical cord. [4] [6] [2] This physiologic herniation is necessary for proper rotation of the intestines and adequate growth of the fetus to house the expanding intestines. [6] The rapid development of the intestines causes elongation of the umbilical cord. [6] Between weeks ten and twelve, the intestines leave the umbilical cord and return to the abdominal cavity. [4] [2] [6] During this time, the extraembryonic mesoderm develops a rich extracellular matrix to protect the cord called Wharton’s jelly. [2] [7] The umbilical cord continues to elongate during the second trimester with a length comparable to the crown-rump length of the fetus. [1] [8] By term, the vitelline duct and allantois have typically completely involuted. [1] [5] However, in some cases, remnants of the allantois and vitelline duct can be found in the umbilical cord proximal to the neonate. [2] [5] At birth, the cord typically measures an average of 50 to 60 cm in length and 2 cm in diameter with up to 40 helical turns. [4] [8] After the birth of the neonate, the umbilical cord is clamped and then cut as the neonate now breathes on its own, and the remainder of the umbilical cord is delivered along with the placenta.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Macrosomia refers to excessive fetal growth, typically defined by an absolute birth weight rather than gestational age, and is an obstetric condition associated with other potentially life-threatening complications to both the mother and the neonate. [1] The term macrosomia is derived from the Greek words macro , meaning big, and somia (body). The earliest use of the term was from the work of Robley Dunglison (1798-1859), an English physician and medical writer. According to the American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology (ACOG), this condition contrasts with "large for gestational age" (LGA), which considers birth weight relative to gestational age, specifically at or above the 90th percentile. Historically, birth weights of 4,000 g or 4,500 g have been used to classify macrosomia, though no universal definition has been accepted. [2] [3] [4] Maternal and neonatal risks increase significantly with higher birth weights. For infants weighing between 4,000 and 4,499 g, a moderate rise in complications, eg, birth injuries and labor abnormalities, have been demonstrated. However, morbidity and mortality risks, including stillbirth and long-term complications like brachial plexus injury, escalate sharply for weights exceeding 4,500 g and are highest above 5,000 g.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Development
retrieved_docs.content: Development is a continuous process from neonatal to adulthood. Though growth ceases after adolescence, adolescence is not the end of development. Each developmental stage has a new set of challenges and opportunities.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Development
retrieved_docs.content: Adolescence: Adolescence is hallmarked by puberty changes, which occur 2 years earlier in females than males. Puberty changes are assessed using the Tanner staging. Acceptance of a new body, separation from home, and establishing oneself as an independent adult are significant challenges in puberty.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Umbilical Cord -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the umbilical cord begins in the third week of embryologic formation. The developing embryo consists of a trilaminar disc attached to the decidua basalis by the connecting stalk, the primitive umbilical cord. [1] [2] The connecting stalk is a thick stalk of the extraembryonic membrane extending from the caudal end of the embryo to the center of the developing placenta on the decidua basalis. [3] The process of body folding occurs during week four with rapid growth amnion and embryonic disc compared to the yolk sac. Cranial caudal folding causes approximation of the connecting stalk and yolk sac on the ventral surface of the embryo. [1] [2] The amnion expands to cover the entire embryo except for the rudimentary umbilical ring, where the connecting stalk and yolk sac emerge. [1] [2] During this time, the allantois, an outpouching of the endodermal hindgut, forms and extends into the connecting stalk. [1] [2] [4] Between the fourth and eighth weeks, there is an increase in amniotic fluid production, which causes the amniotic cavity to swell and fill the chorionic space. This increase in the amniotic fluid also causes elongation of the connecting stalk, and the yolk sac is compressed down within the connecting stalk to form the omphalomesenteric or vitelline duct. [1] [2] [5] The expansion of the amniotic cavity causes the amnion and the chorion to come into contact, and the extraembryonic mesoderm covering these two layers fuses. As such, the chorionic cavity disappears, leaving the umbilical cord, the composite of the connecting stalk and vitelline duct surrounded by the amnion, floating in the amniotic fluid. [1] [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Temperature Regulation -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Newborns rely on nonshivering thermogenesis to metabolize brown adipose tissue (BAT) and release energy. BAT is well-vascularized tissue that develops at the beginning of the third trimester, at approximately week 27 of gestation. BAT is located around the kidneys, adrenal glands, scapulae, axilla, mediastinum, and spine. Newborns are more susceptible to cooling and hypothermia due to their high levels of heat loss by evaporation due to skin immaturity, elevated ratio of body surface area to weight, deficient amounts of subcutaneous adipose tissue for insulation, and poorly developed muscles. Additionally, immature respiratory and circulatory systems may impede proper temperature regulation. There is limited capacity for thermoregulation during the first few weeks of life, especially in preterm and low birthweight infants. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Circadian Rhythm -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the circadian system occurs in mammals postnatally. The fetus is not subjected to external stimuli while in the womb, and thus neonates are born with an immature functioning system. The establishment of the 24-hour circadian rhythms occurs during the first 4 months of life as the newborn experiences rapid physiological changes and adapts to the environment. Since core body temperature is one of the most tightly regulated systems, deviations, among other things, reveal the establishment of circadian rhythm.  Minimal deviations occur in the womb, but in the first few weeks of life, the perception of day and night differences begin. Spikes in core body temperature also begin to manifest just before the onset and first few hours of sleep. Melatonin, critical to the permanent establishment of circadian rhythms, emerges around 3 months of age. The production of cortisol, a key indicator of a properly functioning circadian rhythm, can occur as early as 8 weeks up to 9 months of age. As infants experience rapid physiological changes just after birth, deviations in core body temperature, as well as the production of melatonin and cortisol allow an infant to establish a stable circadian rhythm. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of pregnancy is to allow for the growth and development of the fetus. All changes that occur within the mother’s body are intended to allow for this growth, as well as for the development of the placenta to nourish the fetus and sustain the pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Lanugo -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Lanugo plays an essential role in binding the vernix caseosa to the skin of fetuses. Vernix caseosa is the viscous white covering on newborns that protects their skin, prevents water loss, plays an important role in thermoregulation, and contributes to innate immunity. It protects the fetus from damaging substances found in amniotic fluid, most notably urea and electrolytes. [1] Lanugo is the first type of hair to develop in humans. The interaction of lanugo with the vernix is also important in controlling the tempo of the fetal developmental rate during various times in the gestation cycle. Lanugo arises about 3 months into development. Hair growth starts on the scalp around the eyebrow, nose, and forehead area and proceeds in a cephalocaudal direction from head to toe. It is shed at about 33 to 36 weeks gestation, when it becomes incorporated into the amniotic fluid, eventually contributing to the composition of the meconium. Lanugo is ultimately replaced by vellus (fine, thin hair) and terminal hair (thicker hairs found on the scalp, axilla, and genitalia). Lanugo often remains present in 30% of newborns; this is a normal finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical incompetence (قصور عنق الرحم) is classified across multiple terminologies as a disorder of the female internal genitalia that weakens the cervical canal, often presenting with a patulous cervical os and abnormal uterine cervix morphology. It is a child of “Female internal genitalia structure” and belongs to “Other specified female genital disorders” and the broader group “Abnormality of organs and soft tissues of pelvis complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.” Clinically, cervical incompetence places the patient in a high‑risk pregnancy category and is strongly associated with preterm delivery, low birth weight in infants (INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT), neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة), fetal death (موت الجنين), post‑partum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), and other complications of birth and the puerperium. It often co‑occurs with related obstetric conditions such as polyhydramnios and other amniotic cavity problems, which in turn are linked to pleural

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: also underlies dyspareunia (painful sexual intercourse), which is classified under the same “Other specified female genital disorders” concept. In the pediatric context, infants born preterm with low birth weight have an increased risk of short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature itself is associated with higher rates of neonatal death. Unspecified obstetric codes for care or intervention encompass unspecified malposition or malpresentation, reflecting the hierarchical relationship between broad labor‑delivery indications and specific obstetric complications. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how structural cervical weakness, congenital pelvic anomalies, prior surgical scars, and associated fetal‑maternal outcomes converge to inform risk assessment, diagnosis, and targeted management in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.
